sentences.text: Right.
In that day, they deported 5,000 people and they left 200.
And I was between those 200 that went in with my father.
And delighted he was to see me.
He was just going around like mad, trying to look for me, when I was on the other side.
And we went in to a very small partition in the ghetto that they specially made for the people that they decided to leave.
This was a chance.
I was looking for myself.
I was standing between those people.
And they could not take so much time, because things were going on all the time.
So if they would have found me, they would have shot me.
Right in the place.
In fact, on the Umschlagplatz, the old people, they shot right there.
And they practically discarded the small children.
It was-- you could see that the humanity didn't have-- actually, the Jewish people were not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] treated humanely.
So, of course, as an example they would have shot me.
But this was one of those several lives that I had.
And I managed to survive this incident.
I went into the ghetto, and then another problem started.
The town had 20,000 Jews, and there were four days of deportation-- 5,000 people each day.
Next morning, they took another part from the big ghetto, and they segregated it.
And they also took out all the people that they allowed to stay in that little ghetto the day before in order to check out and be sure that nobody smuggled in that isn't supposed to be there.
My father hid me in a cellar, together with 30 other people.
The Germans later walked around that little ghetto with German shepherds, trying to smell out those people.
I was lucky-- they didn't found our bunker.
My father was up a whole night, and he made a double wall on an attic in that house where we were designated to stay, there, after we came into the small ghetto.
He put feather beds.
There were-- you covered yourself in Poland, with such thick-- like big pillows-- feather beds.
He put feather beds on the attic, and pillows, and other things, to defray the human scent.
Because the feather beds had a human scent, anyhow, and so on.
And he put them around it.
And 12 of us hid there on the attic.
He was going to hide, but he decided to go and check out his legality, because he wouldn't check off.
He would become illegal, too.
He had to keep on staying on the legal list.
The Germans transported 10,000 more people.
They left another 2,000-- 200, excuse me.
Because a lot of the people were workers in the glass factories.
And they need them to operate the production.
In fact, my father would have been very well received if he wanted to go to work in that factory.
He had a very good coverage.
But his pride didn't allow him to go there to work, in a place where he was once such a big shot.
He didn't want to give the satisfaction to some of those people.
So he preferred having that lumpenhandler [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] situation.
They found the cellar, and they shot all the people that were hiding, where I was hiding a day before.
They did not find, thank God, the attic.
Then the third day of deportation, I was again hiding.
The fourth day of deportation that was coming up, that was the last 5,000 people.
My father, in the evening, said, I don't trust those Germans.

I bet you if they are going to run short in number-- because some people ran away, some people hid, and so on-- so they are not going to have the numbers, I bet you they are going to cut off one of the wings that they consider needed or legal, and they are going to send them out.
I am going to hide, too.
But in the last minute, he gave up the idea of hiding, because he wanted to remain legal to be able to help me, say we both are going to be illegal, we are not going to be able to move at all.
And he went out.
And this was the last day of deportation.
And sure, he was right.
They missed some people.
They cut off a wing of those people that were supposed to stay, and they deported them, the last day.
And my father was between them.
And they say that all those transports was from my hometown and from Piotrkow went to Treblinka, which was a camp of just destruction.
So that is how I lost my father, in-- at the end of September, 1942.
And I remained in that small ghetto, illegal.
And prone to all auctions and all-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was also fortunate in the greatest fortune in one thing.
My father emptied his pockets before he went to the-- to be checked out.
And he had some valuable things.
He had some gold dollars and other things.
And he put them in.
And while he was there, waiting for the deportation, he told it to a Jewish policeman that he emptied his pocket.
And where he emptied it, he should go and tell me.
And that man came up to the apartment.
He could have not told me anything.
He could have told everybody to go out, and pick those things up for himself.
He told me, because I think my father knew him or something.
Because this was original his hometown.
And I got those things that really saved my life, again, and that gave me kind of a way to help myself.
And I was there October, November, and December.
In the meantime, I went through--
Oh!
I forgot to say something very important.
When I threw off my coat, I have my Aryan paper, and all kind of codes and tips that were known just to me, in my pocket.
And there I lost my ticket to life.
You couldn't go out of the ghetto and travel, go in in a train, or do anything without the Aryan papers.
I was totally lost.
And no contact with anybody could I reach from the situation that I found myself.
So I was just illegal.
I remember they made me just-- the Jewish community gave me a job to clean the toilets there-- just something that the-- the outhouses.
It was, I would really say, a very difficult time in my life.
I had there a few cousins that survived, but they were very afraid to identify themselves with me.
Because if-- sometimes they had auctions for illegals.
Either they had to denounce me or to take chances that they are going to be taken instead, if they-- if they are not going to find me.
There was one time-- this was December 24-- Christmas Eve-- that the morning, I was caught between 500 people, illegal.
And we were in a temple-- put in in a big temple.
There were even some newborn babies.
And they were lying in long pillows on the podium there, where the cantor used to sing-- crying.
And there was nothing to give them to help them.
And outside were the German soldiers, sitting around a campfire, in the Werkshuhtz, singing holiday songs.
And a couple of the soldiers came in, and they picked up the babies.
And they took the babies, and they threw them in the fire, singing heilige nacht-- holy night.
And I wonder where God was.
They thought that there is not going to be a witness to the cruelty, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] because we were all destined to die.
The 500 people were destined to die.
But in the middle of the night, two Germans came in and called out two names-- my name, and another name.
When you hear a German call your name, you don't want to admit it.
But somebody said, Jadzia, they are calling your name!
So I was in trouble.
I want to make a point.
Before I was caught, that day, in between, I-- when my father was deported, I lived with a bunch of young people.
And they were all Zionist-minded.
And they were-- and they kind of supported me in many ways there, being as I was illegal.
And they were working in the glass factory.
And in that whole place, very few women were legal.
There were 95% men to 5% woman, so I was illegal and a very rare thing, there.
And they all had kind of an allegiance to me.
They lost their mothers, and they lost their sisters, and they lost their wives.
And I was kind of the image of their lost relatives.
And the money that my father-- and the valuable things that my father left me helped me to support myself, so I wasn't really a big burden on them.
And I even cooked something while they were all out at work, and I was there, illegal.
And they even cleaned some of the toilets for me.
I shouldn't have to do that heavy, miserable job.
And the evening before, I had kind of a feeling that I am going to be caught.
So I took out those valuable things, and I said, here you have it-- whatever I had left.
If you can help me with it, help me.
If not, you use it.
It's going to be more handy for you.
Where I would eventually go if I am caught, I won't need those things.
And, sure enough, they bribed two Germans.
And that other woman, they bribed the people from the other woman, too-- together-- they put together.
And those young men not only gave the money and the valuable things that I gave them, some of them gave even things that they had from their mothers.
They were so anxious to-- to save me.
And, sure enough, the two Germans took me out of the temple.
And there were open sewers.
And we went into those sewers.
And they wanted my father's golden watch.
I didn't leave.
This was the only thing I didn't want to part with, sentimentally.
And I gave them the golden watch, so I knew that they must have been bribed.
And I went into the ghetto.
And, of course, I survived with a miracle again.
Next morning, all the 500 people were taken to a forest nearby in Piotrkow.
They were all shot.
And a little time went by, and I was still in that ghetto.
And in January, they caught me again, with a transport of other women that were actually legal, there, too.
And they said that this is going someplace to a working place.
Of course, I didn't believe him, but I was caught, and there was no recourse.
There was nothing to bribe them with, and I had no means.
And-- and I was already tired of that living illegal, day from day, and being dependent and so on.
And, sure enough, lucky for me, that transport didn't go to destruction.
It went to an ammunition factory in Skarzysko-- HASAG.
This was ammunition factories.
This was also in central Poland.
Skarzysko is S-K-A-R-Z-Y-S-K-O.
HASAG.
It was H-A-S-A-G.
It was Werk A.
This was-- they had in Skarzysko three different factories where different people where working at different things.
And I was working at the Werk A.
And I was working by [NON-ENGLISH], which means the shells for the pistol-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
For the pistol-- um-- guns.
These were the small guns-- the little ones.
They were very little, maybe an inch and a half, and a half a centimeter wide and so.
And later on, in a different department, they made the fillings-- the caps for the bullet.
And this was-- that department was making the bullets.
In fact, later on, when I again got my Zionist connections-- we had connections wherever I-- somehow we gravitated to one another, and we found each other.
And we had a contact with-- out of the ghetto, but this was a different contact, through certain Polish people.
And there were also certain groups that went out of the-- not ghetto-- of the camp.
And they were in the bau kommando.
They were in the building kommando, there, and they were building all kind of thing.
And they took out some of the shells, and they dug them in a special place, and they made certain marks.
And some other people took them out.
And from another department--
Very difficult.
We worked 12-hours shifts.
You had to stay on your feet.
And those machines were going on oil.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- they-- they were cutting those shells.
In 36 different measurements, they had to fit, you know?
And that oil made me sores, because my body couldn't adjust to the fat oil.
It was-- it was difficult.
We were living from day to day.
And the rations weren't plentiful.
We got a piece of bread and a soup.
And, as I mentioned before, I kept my bread from day to day, to feel that I am not so hungry.
And the days kind of passed by.
And I say found again a group where we had discussions, later on in the evening, in the camp.
And sometimes we helped each other with a spiritual resistant that was very important for survival.
I don't think I could have made it without it.
In that camp, I was from 1943, January, till 1944, September.
And-- but I got sick.
I got typhus there.
And I almost died.
I was very, very sick.
And then, again, evidently I was meant to live.
The doctor in that hospital-- if you can call it hospital-- there were just such beds where you lied without any medication.
You were in quarantine.
And people were dying like flies.
And-- and they were dying, lying on shelves, and they were dying.
And somehow that main doctor was from my mother's hometown.
And he was in love with one of-- with one of my aunts, and something like that.
Evidently, he knew who I was, and I wasn't just one of the dying sick people.
He treated me specially, so to say.
I got a bed in the middle of the room.
And the other, those [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nurses, I would call them, saw that the doctor knows me, so they treated me a little better, too.
And the son of the doctor also was in love with a cousin of mine-- that he was mourning that she was sent away.
A very beautiful redhead from Katowice.
And she was sent away.
So I remember I was delirious, talking in my temperature.
And I wanted some milk and a bun.
A Kaiser bun.
I used to get it sometimes for breakfast, at home.
And I wanted so a milk and Kaiser.
And, you know, I don't know where he got it for me, but he brought me some milk and a Kaiser bun.
I can't believe it.
Maybe I'm dreaming about it, but I had that milk and Kaiser.
To make the long story short, I survived the typhus.
No, I forgot.
It was a Jewish doctor, and it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his son was there a policeman in the camp.
But they helped me.
They helped me.
Before that, I used to live there in a room.
When we came from Piotrkow, there were 200 people in the same room where I lived, in this HASAG.
And their office was close by, and sometimes they came after and they talked.
And that's how I found out that he was from my mother's hometown, and all those connections.
And it really helped me.
It was the difference between life and death.
And, again, death was so terrible.
They were schlepping the people by the hair-- the dead people-- by their arms, and throw them on piles and took them out, just like garbage.
It was such an inhumane way to die.
That as I say you, you stuck with your nails in.
You didn't even-- this was the most-- the most terrible thing, to see how live people were treated, and how dead people.
After the typhus, I was very weak, and I lost my hair.
I became almost bald.
The hair later on grew back.
And the time passed by.
Very difficult.
People around were dying, and I barely survived.
And later on, in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] September 1944, the Russian army was coming closer to where we were.
And they decided to transport both the factories and the people to Czestochowa.
There are some people that had a radio.
They didn't tell us that that's why they transported us.
You kind of-- certain things, you knew.
We did work with some Polish supervisors.
And there were some of the people that I mentioned that went to outside kommandos, to the building kommandos.
We knew that the front is coming closer.
And we were hanging in there with all our might, and with all our nails to be able to survive.
And when we got transport to Czestochowa-- and that was another ammunition factory--
This is C-Z-E-S-T-O-C-H-O-W-A.
And they also set up the three factories.
I was on Pelcery, which was P-E-L-C-E-R-Y.
And those [NON-ENGLISH] machines.
I was working on them there, too.
I already was a specialist-- to my great pain.
I remember one time-- it fitted on all the measurements-- those [NON-ENGLISH]-- the shells.
But at one little point, there was a little point in the back of that shell like a little tongue, and it had a little scratch.
And that would prevent it to fit the gun.
And I made 25,000 [NON-ENGLISH], which is-- it wasn't usable.
But it fitted on all my measurements.
And I wasn't going to tell it if my Polish supervisor didn't notice it.
And I pretended that I didn't.
And the meister, which means the overseer-- the German overseer-- his name was Knoepfler.
Because he was such a dog, I remember him.
He used to beat people and everything.
Ran by, and he used to grab from the bins, here and there, to check while he ran by.
And he spotted my [NON-ENGLISH].
I got 25 leashes.
Yeah-- lashes.
And for weeks I couldn't sit.
Pardon?
In my bottom.
And I remember I bit my lips to blood, because I did not want to scream too much.
[pauses]
And time was going on, in that Czestochowa, till it started-- I was-- it was my 20th birthday, there-- December 1st.
In the beginning of January, we were wondering-- are we going to see-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are we going to see freedom, or not?
In between us, I mentioned, we had our group.
And I had a very dear friend, [NON-ENGLISH], that if he would have lived out the war, I probably would have married him.
He was from the same organization that I was, too.
And we had a lot of mutual friends in the movement.
And he was a very caring young man.
He was 10 years older than I.
And he was also a great spiritual support for me.
And sometimes he even helped me with a little food.
Because he was the one-- he was-- he was an architect, but in the camp he worked like a glazier, and he worked in the outside kommandos a lot.
And when you worked on the outside kommandos, you could always smuggle in something here and there.
And when we smuggled out the [NON-ENGLISH], I had wooden shoes that had hollowed-out heels--
Those pistol shells.
So I had wooden shoes that had thick wooden heels, and the thing opened up, and I could put in there-- with sawdust in between, it shouldn't rattle.
I couldn't smuggle out a lot. 10, 15 at a time.
But those things counted, and they took it out the same way--
